revision.comment: Bot: remove [[Category:Sn crud]] (crud category cleanup) [[github:EmmaLeonhart/shintowiki-scripts/actions/runs/23984597483|manual triggered pipeline]]
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:هاتشيكازوكي]][[de:Hachikazuki]][[en:Hachikazuki]][[ja:鉢かづき]][[th:แม่นางชามครอบ]][[uz:Hachikazuki]]
{{Short description|Japanese folktale}} [[File:Princess Hachikazuki and Prince Saisho pledge their love.png|thumb|Princess Hachikazuki and Prince Saisho pledge their love]]
'''Hachikazuki''' or '''Hachi Katsugi''' ({{ill|Japanese language|en|Japanese language|qid=Q5287|lt=Japanese|1=Japanese language}}: 鉢かづき; English: "The bowl-bearer princess")<ref name="loc"/><ref name="Seki 1966 115">{{Cite journal|last=Seki|first=Keigo|author-link=Keigo Seki|date=1966|title=Types of Japanese Folktales|url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/1177478|journal=[[Asian Folklore Studies]]|volume=25|issue=1|page=115|doi=10.2307/1177478|jstor=1177478}}</ref> is a [[:en:Japanese folktale|Japanese folktale]] of the ''[[Otogi-zōshi]]'' genre. It refers to a maiden of noble birth who wears a bowl on her head and marries a prince.<ref>Mulhern, Chieko Irie. "[Reviewed Work: Japanese Folk Literature: A Core Collection and Reference Guide. by Joanne P. Algarin]". In: ''Monumenta Nipponica'' 39, no. 2 (1984): 202. Accessed July 24, 2021. doi:10.2307/2385022.</ref>
Hachikazuki hime was first written in the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|qid=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}} (14th-16th centuries).<ref name="loc"/> An akahon (red book) with many drawings for children was published in the middle of the Edo period (18th century, circa 1735-45) by Urokogataya.<ref name="loc">{{cite web |website=Library of Congress |date=January 1, 2009 |title=The Bowl-Bearer Princess |url=https://www.loc.gov/resource/gdcwdl.wdl_14446_002/|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220526021844/https://www.loc.gov/resource/gdcwdl.wdl_14446_002/ |archive-date=May 26, 2022}}</ref>
==Summary== [[File:Sanzōshi-emaki Hachikazuki.jpg|thumb|right|The maiden Hachikazuki, depicted in the style of a Sanzōshi-emaki.]]
In olden times, there lived a couple with a beautiful daughter, sometimes called Hachibime.<ref name="archive.org">Joly, Henri L. ''[https://archive.org/details/legendinjapanese00jolyuoft/page/272/mode/2up Legend in Japanese art; a description of historical episodes, legendary characters, folk-lore myths, religious symbolism]''. London; New York: John Lane, The Bodley Head. 1908. p. 272.</ref> On her deathbed, the girl's elderly mother makes her promise to always wear a wooden bowl on her head to cover her beauty. As an alternate opening, the girl is born with a wooden bowl on her head.<ref name="Seki 1966 115"/> Whatever the case, her father remarries, and her step-mother is cruel to the girl.
The girl escapes from home to another town and employs herself in a menial position in a lord's mansion. One day, the lord's son takes a peek inside the wooden bowl and sees a woman of great beauty. He falls in love with her. When it is time for him to choose a wife, Hachikazuki takes part in the bride selection and is chosen by the youth. The girl says the bowl must stay on her head through the ceremony.
After they marry, the bowl falls from Hachikazuki's head and she discovers it contained jewels and other treasures.<ref name="archive.org"/> Alternatively, the bowl breaks in many pieces and they turn into precious gems.<ref>Cox, Marian Roalfe. ''[https://archive.org/details/cu31924007918299/page/n193/mode/2up Cinderella; three hundred and forty-five variants of Cinderella, Catskin, and Cap o'Rushes]''. London: The Folk-lore Society. 1893. pp. 107-108.</ref>
==Translations== [[File:The violet fairy book (1906) (14751052534).jpg|thumb|right|The lord's son sights the beautiful maiden without her helmet. Art by Henry Justice Ford for ''The Violet Fairy Book'' (1906).]] The tale was translated into {{ill|German language|en|German language|qid=Q188|lt=German language|1=German language}} as ''Das Mädchen mit dem Holznapfe'' ("The Girl with the Wooden Bowl") by David August Brauns ([[:de:David August Brauns|de]]).<ref>Brauns, David August. ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=Y3TEiotn1QEC&pg=PR1 Japanische Märchen und Sagen]''. Leipzig: Verlag von Wilhelm Friedrich, 1885. pp. 74-78.</ref>
In {{ill|English language|en|English language|qid=Q1860|lt=English language|1=English language}} compilations, the tale is known as ''The Wooden Bowl'',<ref>Hearn, Lafcadio et al. ''Japanese fairy tales''. New York: Boni and Liveright. 1918. pp. 80-88.</ref> ''The Black Bowl'',<ref>James, Grace. ''[https://archive.org/details/cu31924008636254/page/n79/mode/1up Green willow and other Japanese fairy tales]''. London: Macmillan. 1912. pp. 56-64.</ref> ''The Maiden with the Wooden Bowl'',<ref>Davis, Frederick Hadland; Paul, Evelyn. ''[https://archive.org/details/mythslegendsofja00davi/page/317/mode/1up Myths & legends of Japan]''. London: George G. Harrap. 1912. pp. 317-322.</ref> or ''The Maiden with the Wooden Helmet'' ({{ill|Andrew Lang|en|Andrew Lang|qid=Q505827|lt=Andrew Lang|1=Andrew Lang}}'s translation).<ref>Lang, Andrew. ''[https://archive.org/details/violetfairybook00lang/page/270/mode/1up The Violet Fairy Book]''. London; New York: Longmans, Green. 1906. pp. 270-274.</ref>
==Distribution== Folklorist [[:en:Seki Keigo|Seki Keigo]] listed three published sources where the tale appears: one from {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|en|Saitama Prefecture|qid=Q128186|lt=Saitama|1=Saitama Prefecture}}; another from [[:en:Hyōgo|Hyōgo]] and the third from [[:en:Tokushima|Tokushima]].<ref name="Seki 1966 115"/>
==Analysis== Folklorist {{ill|Marian Roalfe Cox|en|Marian Roalfe Cox|qid=Q6761971|lt=Marian Roalfe Cox|1=Marian Roalfe Cox}}, in her work ''Cinderella: Three Hundred and Forty-Five Variants of Cinderella, Catskin and, Cap O' Rushes'', listed ''Hachihazuki'' ("The Girl with the Wooden Bowl") as belonging to the cycle of ''{{ill|Cinderella|en|Cinderella|qid=Q11841|lt=Cinderella|1=Cinderella}}'' stories,<ref>Mayer, Fanny Hagin. "Reviewed Work: 越後のシンデレラ by 水沢謙一" [''Echigo no Shinderera'' by Kenichi Mizusawa]. In: ''Asian Folklore Studies'' 24, no. 1 (1965): 151. Accessed July 25, 2021. doi:10.2307/1177604.</ref> albeit of a indeterminate affiliation.<ref>Cox, Marian Roalfe. ''[https://archive.org/details/cu31924007918299/page/n193/mode/2up Cinderella; three hundred and forty-five variants of Cinderella, Catskin, and Cap o'Rushes]''. London: The Folk-lore Society. 1893. pp. 107-108, 177.</ref> In the same vein, professor Chieko Irie Mulhern lists ''Hachikazuki'' as part of the Japanese Cinderella cycle, which she states has been introduced and developed by literary works of Jesuits.<ref>{{cite journal |last=Mulhern |first=Chieko Irie |title=Cinderella and the Jesuits. An Otogizōshi Cycle as Christian Literature |journal=Monumenta Nipponica |volume=34 |issue=4 |date=1979 |pages=409–410 |doi=10.2307/2384103|jstor=2384103 }} Accessed January 12, 2023.</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last=Mulhern |first=Chieko Irie |title=Analysis of Cinderella Motifs, Italian and Japanese |journal=Asian Folklore Studies |volume=44 |issue=1 |date=1985 |pages=2, 6, 16–20 |doi=10.2307/1177981 |jstor=1177981}} Accessed January 12, 2023.</ref>
Japanese scholar Hiroko Ikeda classified the tale, translated as ''Princess Bowl-on-the-Head'', as type 510C of her Japanese tale index,<ref>Hiroko Ikeda. ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=EPgZAAAAIAAJ&q=bowl A Type and Motif Index of Japanese Folk-Literature]''. Folklore Fellows Communications Vol. 209. Helsinki: Suomalainen Tiedeakatemia. 1971. p. 140.</ref> thus close to other ''Persecuted Heroine'' types in the [[:en:Aarne-Thompson-Uther Index|Aarne-Thompson-Uther Index]]: ATU 510A, "Cinderella", and ATU 510B, "{{ill|Donkeyskin|en|Donkeyskin|qid=Q835505|lt=Donkeyskin|1=Donkeyskin}}".
==Adaptations== The tale was adapted as ''Lily and the Wooden Bowl'', by Alan Schroeder in 1994.<ref>"Lily and the Wooden Bowl (Book Review)". In: ''PublishersWeekly''. Date: 09/01/1994. Retrieved: July 24th, 2021. Url: https://www.publishersweekly.com/978-0-385-30792-5</ref>
The tale was used as inspiration for the novel ''Six Crimson Cranes'' by Elizabeth Lim.
== References == {{reflist}}
==Further reading== *小林 健二 (2007). "御伽草子「鉢かづき」諸本における本文の流動と固定--宰相の乳母と嫁比べの進言者をめぐって" [On the text-variation of the Hachikazuki stories: a study of the characterization and roles of Saisho's nurse and the advisor of the bride-contest]. In: ''The Journal of Kokugakuin University'' 108 (7): 13-23. {{ISSN|0288-2051}}. https://ci.nii.ac.jp/naid/40015514802/en/
{{Cinderella}} {{Japanese folklore long}}
